title: n8n Training Tutorial
description: Step-by-step guide to mastering n8n workflows

content.value:
Introduction

Welcome to the n8n training tutorial! This comprehensive guide will teach you everything you need to know about building powerful automation workflows.

What is n8n?

The Core Problem: How can applications automatically talk to each other without a human acting as the middleman?

The Answer: n8n is a workflow automation platform with a visual drag-and-drop interface. Build workflows by connecting nodes together.

Key Features

  • Open-source & Self-host — Run on your own server, full data control
  • No-code + Code — Drag & drop or write JS/Python
  • 400+ Integrations — Gmail, Slack, Sheets, Telegram, and more
  • Data = JSON — Every node receives and returns JSON

What You'll Learn

  • Core n8n concepts and terminology
  • Creating and managing workflows
  • Working with nodes and connections
  • Handling data and expressions
  • Best practices for production workflows

What is a Workflow?

A workflow in n8n is a sequence of connected nodes that process data. Each node performs a specific action, and data flows between nodes.

Let's look at a simple "Hello World" workflow:

content.alt: Hello World workflow in n8n visual editor showing two connected nodes

content.value:
This workflow has two nodes connected together:

  • Manual Trigger: Starts the workflow when you click 'Execute'
  • Edit Fields: Sets a message variable with the value "Hello from n8n!"

The connections section defines how data flows from the trigger to the Edit Fields node. After execution, you can see the output shows "Hello from n8n!" in the results panel.

Node Basics

Nodes are the building blocks of n8n workflows. Each node has inputs, outputs, and configuration parameters.

Common Node Types

Node TypeKey QuestionExamples
Trigger NodeWhen does the workflow run?Webhook, Schedule, Gmail Trigger, Manual Trigger
Action NodeWhat to do with the data?Google Sheets, Slack, HTTP Request, Gmail Send, Edit Fields
Logic NodeHow does data flow?IF (branching), Merge (combine), Code (JS/Python)

Example: Edit Fields Node

Let's examine the Edit Fields node from our Hello World workflow:

content.alt: Edit Fields node configuration panel in n8n showing parameters and output

content.value:
Node Structure Explained

  • name: Human-readable node name ("Edit Fields")
  • type: Node type identifier (n8n-nodes-base.set)
  • position: Visual placement on the canvas [x, y]
  • parameters: Configuration settings for the node
  • id: Unique identifier for the node

In this example, the node creates a new field called "message" with the value "Hello from n8n!" The screenshot above shows how you configure this in the n8n interface - you can see the Parameters tab where the field is set, and the Output panel showing the resulting data.

How Nodes Talk

Now that you understand nodes, let's see how data flows between them. The core concept: Every node passes JSON to the next node. Connect nodes together and you create a data pipeline.

This workflow demonstrates the complete journey—from creating data to transforming it:

content.alt: User processing workflow showing data flow through multiple nodes

content.value:
The Data Journey

Create Sample Users → Split Into Items (1 array becomes 2 items) → Transform Data (add new fields to each item)

After splitting, each item flows through as a separate JSON object:

Expressions in Action

How the Transform Data node accesses and manipulates each item:

3 Rules to Remember

RuleExplanation
1 item = 1 JSON objectEach object is processed separately through the workflow. If you have 100 items, the workflow runs 100 times internally.
Array = batch processingn8n loops through items automatically—no extra code needed. The Split Into Items node converts an array into individual items.
Access with expressionsUse {{ $json.email }} to get "alice@test.com" for the first item. The $json variable always refers to the current item being processed.

The Language of Data

Expressions are how you talk to your data in n8n. Think of them like an address system to find the right room in a building of JSON.

Building (Workflow) → Floor (Node) → Room (Field) → Value

In step 4, you saw {{ $json.email }} in action. Now let's understand the full expression system.

Expression Syntax

Wrap any expression in double curly braces with a space:

{{ $json.fieldName }}       // Access a field
{{ $json.age + 5 }}          // Do math
{{ $json.name.toUpperCase() }} // Use JavaScript methods
{{ $json.role === 'admin' ? 'Full Access' : 'Limited' }} // Conditionals
Inside {{ }}, you write JavaScript. n8n evaluates it and inserts the result.

The 4 Variable Groups

n8n provides 4 ways to access data. Each serves a different purpose:

VariableWhat It DoesWhen to Use
$jsonData from the previous nodeMost common—use by default
$('Node Name')Data from any specific nodeWhen you need data from a node that's not directly before
$inputCurrent node's input dataAdvanced—loops, multiple inputs
$now / $todayCurrent date/timeTimestamps, date filtering, logging

Quick Examples

Here's how to use each variable type:

Expressions in Action: Live Demo

Here's how all these expressions work together in a real workflow:

content.alt: Complete workflow showing all 5 nodes with expression outputs in the data table
Add Metadata node detail view showing input data, expression parameters, and output results

content.value:
Making Decisions

Now that you can access and transform data, it's time to control where it goes. Logic nodes let your workflow make decisions and route items down different paths.

The Key Question: Based on the data, which path should this item take?

The IF Node

Split your workflow into two paths: true or false. Use when you have a yes/no decision.

// Example conditions:
{{ $json.role === 'admin' }}              // Is user an admin?
{{ $json.age >= 18 }}                     // Is user 18 or older?
{{ $json.price > 100 }}                   // Is price over $100?
{{ $json.status === 'active' }}           // Is status active?
{{ $json.email.includes('@company.com') }} // Is company email?
Result: Items flow down the true path or false path based on your condition.

The Switch Node

Route items to multiple paths based on a value. Use when you have 3+ possible outcomes.
Result: Each item goes to the matching output path. Unmatched items go to the default path.

The Merge Node

Combine multiple paths back into one. Use after branching when you want to process all items together again.

Example: Our demo workflow merges admin and user paths, then sends a single Gmail summary with all processed users.

Quick Comparison

NodeOutputsUse When
IF2 (true/false)Binary decision — yes or no, include or exclude
SwitchMultipleRoute by value — status, category, type, etc.
Merge1Combine paths back together after branching
Filter1Remove items that don't match condition

Logic in Action: User Role Router

This workflow demonstrates the complete branching pattern: split paths based on conditions, process differently, merge back together, and send a unified result.

content.alt: Complete workflow showing branching, merging, and Gmail integration with 7 nodes
Split Into Items node converting 1 array into 3 separate user items
IF node detail showing condition check and true/false branch outputs
Merge node showing append mode combining 2 admin items and 1 user item into 3 total items
Gmail node showing HTML message template and successful email sends for all 3 users
Actual Gmail inbox showing the User Access Summary Report with all 3 users listed with their access levels and badges

content.value:
How It Works

  1. Create Sample Users — Generates 3 users (2 admins, 1 regular user)
  2. Split Into Items — Each user becomes a separate item
  3. IF: Is Admin? — Checks if {{ $json.role === 'admin' }}
  4. True Path → Admin Actions — Admins get "Full Access" + 👑 badge
  5. False Path → User Actions — Regular users get "Limited Access" + 👤 badge
  6. Merge — Combines both paths back into one stream (3 items total)
  7. Gmail Send Summary — Sends one email with all users listed

Result: Alice and Charlie (admins) + Bob (user) all appear in a single summary email showing their access levels and badges.

Note: To run this workflow, you'll need to configure Gmail credentials in n8n. Update the email address in the Gmail node to your own.
